title: Efficient methodology for the extraction and analysis of lipids from porcine pulmonary artery by supercritical fluid chromatography coupled to mass spectrometry
author: Alicia Gil-Ramirez
license_note: Zenodo record metadata license field: cc-by-4.0
description:
This manuscript compares and discusses the efficiency of six extraction methods to recover lipids from pulmonary artery. Discussions are based on relative quantification data from gravimetric and mass spectrometric analysis. Results showed that triacylglycerides, phospholipids, monoacylglycerides, sphimgomyelins, fatty acids and sterols (cholesterol) were the six lipid classes chromatographically separated in under nine minutes. According to the considered nominative and numeric parameters, a dichloromethane-based extraction method was found to be the most efficient one, while a MTBE-based extraction method was the one showed the highest fatty acid extraction efficiency. Finally, it was observed that the extraction method greatly affected the fragmentation of monoglycerides in the ion source. 

 This work is not only intended to bring new knowledge about lipid composition of porcine pulmonary arteries, but also to provide with reliable tools for further lipidomic studies in blood vessels. Our work has implications in the field of tissue transplantation. This manuscript is based on the premise that developing a selective process to remove lipids without damaging the extracellular matrix greatly depends on knowing the amount and type of lipid compounds in the specific tissue. The amount and type of lipids determined depends in turn on the performance of the extraction method. Furthermore, reliability of the extraction method is of utmost importance and not always evaluated. Our results show that while the identified lipids were the same for all extracts, the extracted amounts differed significantly depending on the extraction method.
